editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 11 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: ¶ The foregoing is confirmed by Paul, Romans 10. There is no distinction between Jew and Greek. And in Galatians 3: There is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, neither male nor female: for all are one in Christ. And in Romans 1 he acknowledges himself bound to preach the Gospel to all: To Greeks and barbarians, to the wise and the unwise, I am a debtor: and, as much as is in me, I am ready to preach the gospel also to you who are in Rome. Again, blessed Peter in Acts 10: Peter opened his mouth and said: In truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons, but in every nation he who fears God and works righteousness is acceptable to him. Where the gloss of Bede says that God is no respecter of persons, because he sent his only-begotten Son — who is the creator and lord of all — to make peace with the human race, in whose name, as the prophets testify, not only Jews but all believers would receive the remission of sins. Thus far he.

To all peoples, therefore, without distinction, the gospel must be proclaimed in a brotherly and friendly manner, offering them the remission of sins. So the blessed Ambrose, Book 2, Chapter 1, On the Calling of All Nations says: Has any distinction of nations or of persons been made by this commandment? It excluded no one by merit, it separated no one by race, it made no distinction by condition. And to sum it up, the blessed Ambrose there teaches that the grace of God has shone upon all people of every nation, and that the messenger of the gospel has been and must be sent to all — to some sooner, to others later — since, as he firmly declares: it must be professed that God wills all people to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth. Thus far he.

Therefore peace, Christian friendship, and mutual love must be offered to the pagans to whom the Gospel is first proclaimed, together with the offer of the remission of sins. I call "the first proclamation" that which is made to peoples who have nowhere yet heard the Gospel. The second will be called that which is made to heretics and schismatics — just as the "first Eucharist" is said to be that which Christ instituted, as is clear in decretis 1. q. 1, c. christus quid fecit where it is written: that he would hand over to him the first Eucharist, consecrated by his own hands and commended by his own mouth, as he had given it to the other apostles.

¶ Third, from those words of Christ, namely for so it is written, we must note that just as the Gospel must be proclaimed in the same manner and form to Greeks as to Latins, to Scythians, barbarians, slave and free, men and women — so too no distinction is to be made on the grounds that some people are subject to the most atrocious crimes or to however dreadful vices of idolatry or other abominable kinds. For the greater the crimes of the pagans, the more brightly will shine the immense mercy of God, when pagans see the proclaimers of the Gospel offering the remission of all crimes, whatever they may be and of whatever kind.

This will greatly move them to take up the light and gentle yoke of Christ, as the gloss there teaches. For if the Gospel was proclaimed to those who crucified Christ — than which crime none more atrocious can be imagined — offering them the remission of sins, who will be so foolish as to imagine even in a dream that there are any pagans, however impious and abominable, to whom the Gospel should not be proclaimed in the same form, with the offered indulgence and remission of sins and fraternal charity? So the gloss notes there that it was necessary that the law of Christ first be preached to the Jews gently and kindly, so that the gentiles — entangled in various errors and misdeeds — might by this very proof of divine mercy be summoned to hope for pardon, seeing that pardon is granted even to those who crucified the Son. Thus far it.

This is more clearly evident from the first sermon that Peter delivered to the Jews after receiving the Holy Spirit. For he addressed them thus: Let all the house of Israel therefore know most assuredly that God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Christ. On hearing this they were pierced to the heart and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles: What shall we do, brethren? But Peter said to them: Repent, and let each one of you be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit, and so forth. Acts 2.

Where it is written that three thousand of those people received the faith that day and persevered in the teaching of the apostles. Likewise, when Peter was doubting whether all nations were capable of the kingdom of God, and whether they should be admitted to the grace of the Gospel indifferently, or whether some should be excluded on account of the enormity of their crimes, a sheet was shown to him by divine revelation containing all kinds of unclean and terrifying creatures — serpents and birds — and when he was told: Peter, kill and eat, and Lord, I have never eaten anything unclean was his reply, it was said to him: Do not call unclean what God has made clean. Wherefore, understanding that no sinner, however great, ought to be rejected from divine grace, he said: Now in truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons, but in every nation he who fears him and works righteousness is acceptable to him. God sent the word to the children of Israel, proclaiming peace through Jesus Christ.

And Jerome, in the Epistle to Augustine, which is number 170, speaking of this vision of Peter, says: A voice was made to Peter: Arise, Peter, kill and eat, and so forth. By which word it is shown that no human being is by nature polluted, but that all are equally called to the Gospel of Christ. Because the voice to Peter was: what God has cleansed, do not you call common, that is, unclean. Thus far Jerome.

There were moreover at Corinth men infamous for impiety, sodomy, and robbery; yet Paul wrote them so many letters with paternal charity, and proclaimed the Gospel to them in no different form than to other peoples — indeed he brought them to Christ with greater gentleness and greater kindness and meekness, going so far as to labor with his hands lest he be a burden to those greedy people. And so in 2 Corinthians 11 he says: that I preached the gospel of God to you free of charge, robbing other churches by accepting support for the purpose of serving you, and when I was with you and was in need, I was not a burden to any of you and so forth.

That this people was subject to abominable crimes is proved by the words of the apostle, who rebukes and admonishes them in 1 Corinthians 6 saying: Do not be deceived: neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor the sexually immoral, nor sodomites, nor thieves, nor the covetous, nor drunkards, nor the abusive, nor robbers will possess the kingdom of God. And such were some of you: but you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and in the Spirit of our God — thus far he. Can there be vices that surpass these, either in number or in gravity? By no means.

Therefore there is no crime so dreadful — whether of idolatry, sodomy, or any other kind — that causes the Gospel to have to be preached differently in the first proclamation thereof: which has been instituted by Christ, namely by fraternal charity, offering the remission of sins, and exhorting men to repentance. For it would be to overturn the form established by Christ.

Therefore it does not belong to the Church, on account of idolatry or any other however grave crimes, to begin the first preaching of the faith with punishment — but rather with exhortation to repentance and the offer of remission of all sins, by virtue of divine clemency.

¶ In agreement with these things is what Almain writes in his book On Ecclesiastical Power, chapter 21: that the Pope cannot punish a pagan who commits some crime — even if the pagan knows it to be a sin — such as the sin of idolatry, or sin against nature, or stealing the property of others. Almain cites Paul, who, after listing idolaters, drunkards, the abusive, and thieves, added: what have I to do with judging those who are outside?

On this basis Almain censures Panormitanus in c. Novit. de iudiciis, who teaches that the Pope can punish a pagan who sins against the law of nature — though the common opinion of the doctors is to the contrary. From this Almain infers that no pagan is bound to avoid a Christian who has been excommunicated, since the Pope has no jurisdiction over a pagan. But in truth Panormitanus does not teach that the Pope can castigate him, but rather denounce, urge, and fraternally correct him, according to the precept of Christ. But even on this point Panormitanus is censured by Barbatius in Decretalibus c. Novit. n. 32, who teaches that fraternal correction enjoined upon us by Christ does not oblige us to use it with pagans — and he brings good arguments for this.

Whatever the case may be, Panormitanus is speaking about Jews who sin against their own law — on account of what he adds, namely, if they sin against their own law. As for other pagans — Saracens or idolaters — who sin against their own laws, which are foolish and irrational, a Christian is not bound to correct them in order that they might not transgress those laws. First, because to do so would be to approve their unjust and impious laws. Second, because no Christian — given that there are so many pious and holy people in the Church — does or cares to do any such thing, which is a strong argument that we are not obligated to do so in such matters. In the case of Jews, however, of whom the Abbot speaks, there is a reason for a difference. For a Jew who sins against his own law in moral matters sins against the divine law, which we ourselves profess and acknowledge; and consequently he commits an injury against the Christian Church, which can coerce and punish Jews as its own subjects at will.

But if the Abbot also meant his opinion to apply to other infidels, it must be admitted in the case of pagans who are by right and in fact subject to Christian rulers. And in this sense must be understood what Innocent teaches in c. quod super his. de voto: that the Pope can punish infidels who sin against the law of nature, or Jews who commit something contrary to the Gospel in moral matters — that is, insofar as they act against the divine law of the Old Testament which they themselves profess, by mingling certain heresies — provided they are not already punished by their own leaders. And for this reason Innocent reports that Pope Gregory and Pope Innocent had the books of the Talmud burned, in which many heresies were contained, and they commanded that those who taught and followed those foolish fables should be punished. Thus far from Innocent, whose teaching rests on the ground that Jews are subjects of the Church and of Christian rulers. Concerning which matter we shall speak at greater length below.

In sum, no pagan, on account of any crime or superstition however abominable, or any sin however grave, committed strictly within the limits of their own lords' territory and within their own unbelief, can be punished by the Church — and far less by Christian rulers. For there is no ruler or judge to whom the care of all the peoples of the entire world belongs, except to the Church alone and to the vicar of Jesus Christ, who is the Roman Pontiff — to invite and attract pagans to the faith, and to protect Catholics and confirm them in the truth of the faith.
